1: wayyaʿan ṣôp̄ar hannaʿᵃmāṯî wayyōʾmar: 'Then Zophar the Naamathite answered and said.' Zophar's second and final speech (Job 20) is his response to Job's great Redeemer declaration (Job 19:25-27). His reply does not engage Job's vision of vindication; instead he reaches past it to reassert the classical retribution doctrine with escalating rhetorical ferocity. The deliberate non-engagement with Job's confession is revealing: the friends are unable to process a claim that exceeds their theological framework.
2: lāḵēn śēʿîpāy yᵉšîḇûnî ûḇaʿăḇûr ḥûšî bî: 'Therefore my thoughts push me to answer; I feel urgency within me.' Śāʿap̄ (thoughts that agitate, troubled reflections — from śāʿap̄ = to split, divide) — Zophar's inner turmoil drives the speech. His urgency is presented as intellectual compulsion rather than compassionate response to Job's suffering. The contrast with Job's anguished speech (Job 19) is sharp: where Job's emotion was grief and longing, Zophar's is irritated argument.
3: mûsar kelimmaṯî ʾešmaʿ wᵉrûaḥ mibbinatî yaʿănênî: 'I hear a reproof that dishonors me, and a spirit from beyond my understanding compels me.' Kelimmāh (dishonor, shame, humiliation) — Zophar feels personally shamed by Job's arguments. Rûaḥ mibbinatî: 'a spirit from beyond/out of my understanding' — Zophar claims to be moved by something beyond his normal rational process, adding prophetic authority to his speech. Yet the content of his speech is entirely conventional; the claim of spiritual compulsion underscores how readily such claims are made in the defense of traditional theology.
4: hă-zōʾt yāḏaʿtā minnî-ʿad minnî śîm ʾādām ʿălê-ʾāreṣ: 'Do you not know this from of old, since man was placed upon the earth?' Zophar opens his argument by appealing to tradition — the appeal to antiquity (minnî-ʿad = from of old, from then on) is the standard wisdom-school move. The appeal to what 'everyone has always known' insulates the argument from critical examination. Job has already challenged this move (Job 12:3, 9-10) by pointing to the observable world contradicting the traditional teaching.
5: kî rinnāt rᵉšāʿîm miqārôḇ wᵉśimḥat ḥānēp̄ ʿăḏê-rāgaʿ: 'That the triumph of the wicked is short, and the joy of the godless lasts but a moment.' Rinnāh (triumphal shout, ringing cry of joy) — the emotional peak of the wicked's prosperity. ʿĂḏê-rāgaʿ: 'until a moment' — the brevity is emphatic. Zophar's assertion about the wicked's brief prosperity is the central claim of his speech; Job will systematically refute it in Job 21 by pointing to the observable fact that the wicked often live long and prosper to old age.
6: ʾim-yaʿăleh laššāmayim śiʾô wᵉrōʾšô lāʿāḇ yaggiʿa: 'Though his greatness mounts to the heavens and his head reaches the clouds.' The imagery of sky-high ambition echoes the tower of Babel (Genesis 11:4) and prophetic mockery of Babylon (Isaiah 14:13-14). The wicked man's vertical ambition — his claim to heaven-touching greatness — is the setup for the fall in Job 20:7. The contrast between Job 20:6 (sky-height) and Job 20:7 (dung) is deliberately grotesque: the height of human self-assertion collapses into the most degraded imagery.
7: kᵉgāllô lānetsaḥ yōʾḇēḏ rōʾāyw yōʾmᵉrû ʾayyô: 'He will perish forever like his own dung; those who saw him will say, “Where is he?”' Gāllāh (his dung, his own excrement) — one of the Bible's most blunt uses of scatological language to illustrate the worthlessness of the wicked's legacy. The fall from cloud-height (Job 20:6) to dung (Job 20:7) is the sharpest possible contrast. The rhetorical question ʾayyô (where is he?) echoes the divine question to Cain (Genesis 4:9) and becomes a funeral formula: what once seemed great has vanished without a trace.
8: kᵉḥălôm yāʿûp̄ wᵉlōʾ yimṣāʾûhû wayyûḏad kᵉḥezyôn lāylāh: 'He flies away like a dream and cannot be found; he is chased away like a vision of the night.' The dream/night-vision double comparison emphasizes insubstantiality: both are experiences of reality that dissolve upon waking. Ḥalôm (dream) and ḥezyôn lāylāh (vision of the night) are related but distinct terms. The wicked man's greatness is phenomenological — subjectively experienced as real but ontologically empty, gone when the dawn of judgment comes.
9: ʿayin šᵉzāp̄athu wᵉlōʾ-tôsîp̄ wᵉlōʾ-ʿôḏ tᵉšûrennû māqômô: 'The eye that saw him will see him no more; his own place will no longer recognize him.' The personification of the wicked man's place (māqôm) as having memory — the place that knew him will no longer recognize him — adds cosmic weight. The language echoes the prophetic passages where the land mourns the absence of the departed wicked (Isaiah 14:16-17) and anticipates Revelation's erasure of the wicked from the book of life.
10: bānāyw yᵉraṣṣû ḏallîm wᵉyāḏāyw tāšêṣnāh ʾônô: 'His children will seek favor from the poor, and his own hands will give back their wealth.' The reversal extends to the next generation: the children of the formerly prosperous wicked are reduced to begging from the poor — the exact people their father oppressed. Tāšêṣnāh ʾônô: 'his hands will restore/give back his wealth' — the restitution principle: ill-gotten gain is returned, whether by compulsion or divine judgment. The children of the wicked inherit not wealth but obligation.
11: ʿăṣāmōṯāyw mālaʾû ʿălûmôṯāyw wᵉʿimmô ʿal-ʿāp̄ār tiškāḇ: 'His bones are full of the sins of his youth; they will lie down with him in the dust.' ʿĂlûmôt (youthful vigor, the vitality of youth) — his bones are saturated with the stored-up sin-energy of his youth; when he dies, this accumulated sin lies down with him. The image presents sin as a physical substance that inhabits the body — an almost material conception of moral corruption. The dust-bed destination (ʿāp̄ār) connects to the creation-and-death framework of Genesis 3:19.
12: ʾim-tāʾēraḇ bᵉp̄îw rāʿāh yaḵᵉḥîḏennāh taḥat lᵉšônô: 'Though evil is sweet in his mouth and he hides it under his tongue.' The sweetness-of-evil imagery has wide resonance: Proverbs 9:17 ('stolen water is sweet'), Ezekiel 3:3 (the scroll sweet in the prophet's mouth). The hiding under the tongue is a culinary metaphor — savoring, like wine held under the tongue before swallowing. The pleasure precedes the poison. Tāʾab (to be sweet) connects to the enticement of sin that Proverbs consistently addresses: evil presents itself as desirable before revealing its destructive nature.
13: yaḥmōl ʿāleyhā wᵉlōʾ yaʿazᵉḇennāh waymannaʿennāh bᵉṯôḵ ḥikkô: 'Though he spares it and will not let it go, but keeps it lodged in his palate.' The extended oral-retention metaphor (Job 20:12-14) presents the wicked man as unable to release what he knows will destroy him — gripping pleasurable evil in his mouth even as it turns. Ḥēḵ (palate, roof of the mouth) — the most sensory part of tasting. The man is so habituated to the sweetness of evil that he cannot spit it out even when its venom is revealed. The structural parallel to addiction is striking.
14: laḥmô bᵉmēʿāyw nehpāḵ mᵉrôrat pᵉtānîm bᵉqirbô: 'Yet his food turns in his stomach; it becomes cobra venom (rōʾš, mᵉrôrat pᵉtānîm) inside him.' Rōʾš (gall, bitter herb, venom) and mᵉrôrat pᵉtānîm (venom of cobras) — cobra poison was one of the most feared in the ANE. The reversal of sweetness to venom inside the body is Zophar's central biological metaphor for retribution: what was consumed as pleasure becomes the poison that destroys from within. This is moral physiology — the nature of sinful pleasure is self-destructive at a fundamental level.
15: ḥayil bālaʿ wayyᵉqîʾennû mibbiṭnô yôrišennû ʾēl: 'He swallowed wealth down and will vomit it up again; God will expel it from his belly.' Bālaʿ (swallow, engulf) — a violent consuming. Qîʾ (vomit up) is rare and deliberately crude. God is the active agent of the expulsion: what was consumed as wealth is ejected by divine force. The image is the inverse of receiving a divine gift: God gives by provision, but what is seized unjustly God takes back by extraction. The stomach as the seat of greed that cannot retain its ill-gotten contents.
16: rōʾš-pᵉtānîm yinqāq waharg-lô leššôn ʾep̄ʿeh: 'He will suck the venom of cobras; the tongue of the viper will kill him.' The cobra imagery continues: what he swallowed as food turns out to be venom; what he sucks seeking nourishment is poison. The peten (asp/cobra) and ʾep̄ʿeh (viper) are two distinct venomous snakes. The tongue of the viper — the instrument of both deception and injection — is the agent of his death. The serpent imagery connects to the Eden narrative (Genesis 3:1-5) where serpentine deception first presented evil as sweet and desirable.
17: ʾal-yirʾeh bipelaggôt nᵉhārôt naḥălê devāš waḥemʾāh: 'He will not look upon the rivers, the flowing channels, the streams of honey and butter.' The positive imagery of abundance — honey, butter, flowing rivers — is denied to the wicked. This is a promised-land refusal: the wicked man is excluded from the eretz zāḇat ḥālāḇ ûḏᵉḇaš (land flowing with milk and honey, Exodus 3:8) that represents YHWH's provision. What the righteous inherit, the wicked are barred from — not by geography but by the moral logic of their own choices.
18: mᵉšîḇ yāgāʿ wᵉlōʾ yiḇlāʿ ḵᵉḥêl tᵉmûrāṯô wᵉlōʾ yaʿălōs: 'He must restore the fruits of his labor without consuming them; his restitution will be according to his wealth, and he shall not enjoy it.' The principle of šāḇ (restoring/returning) — what he earned by exploitation he must give back. The commercial vocabulary (yāgāʿ = labor, toil; tᵉmûrāh = exchange value, barter price) makes this a legal financial restitution. The double negative pleasure-denial: he returns what he earned and cannot even enjoy the process of exchange.
19: kî-rāṣaṣ ʿāzaḇ dallîm bêt gāzal wᵉlōʾ yiḇnēhû: 'For he oppressed and abandoned the poor; he seized a house he did not build.' The ethical charge that grounds the punishment: rāṣaṣ (to crush, oppress, break) the poor — the word used in Isaiah 58:6 ('untie every yoke') and Luke 4:18 ('to set the oppressed free' — rᵉṣûṣîm). He seized (gāzal = take by force, rob) a house he did not build — the Naboth's vineyard dynamic (1 Kgs 21) where the powerful seize what belongs to the vulnerable. The punishment (Job 20:20-28) is proportioned to the offense.
20: kî lōʾ yāḏaʿ šāleū bᵉbiṭnô bᵉḥămûḏô lōʾ yimmalēṭ: 'He will not feel peace (šālem) within; he will retain nothing of what he desired.' Šālem (peace, completeness, wholeness — shalom root) within his belly: the man who seized prosperity by violence cannot experience the inner wholeness that is the organic result of righteous living. Ḥāmaḏ (desire, covet) — the verb of the tenth commandment (Exodus 20:17; Deuteronomy 5:21). Coveting generates restlessness, not satisfaction: the very desire-structure that drove the acquisition undermines the enjoyment of it.
21: ʾên-śārîḏ lᵉʾoḵlô ʿal-kēn lōʾ-yāḥîl ṭûḇô: 'Nothing is left of his food; therefore no one will inherit his prosperity.' Śārîḏ (survivor, remnant) — the word used for the remnant of Israel (Isaiah 1:9; Job 10:20; Joel 2:32). When nothing survives of what he consumed, there is no legacy. The theological irony: the man who consumed and hoarded has no remainder — no remnant of food, no remnant of children to inherit. The family-legacy termination is Zophar's most pointed threat, striking at the ANE's deepest investment: perpetuation through heirs.
22: bimᵉlōʾ śipqô yēṣar lô kol-yāḏ ʿāmēl tāḇôʾ-ʿālāyw: 'In the fullness of his plenty he will be in distress; every hand of the suffering will come against him.' The paradox: his maximum prosperity coincides with maximum distress. Śipeq (plenty, sufficiency, fullness) — he achieves abundance; at that exact moment (yēṣar lô = he will be pressed, in straits) the distress arrives. The phrase reverses the normal expectation: fullness brings comfort, not anguish. The arriving hands of the ʿāmēl (those who toil/suffer) are the oppressed he once crushed, now rising against him.
23: yᵉhî lᵉmalēʾ biṭnô yᵉšallaḥ-bô ḥărôn ʾappô wayyamṭēr ʿālāyw bilᵉḥûmô: 'When he is set to fill his belly, God will hurl his fierce anger upon him and rain it down on him as he eats.' Šālaḥ ḥărôn ʾap̄ (send/hurl fierce anger) — divine wrath as a thrown weapon. Māṭar (to rain) — used of the Sodom judgment (Genesis 19:24: 'YHWH rained sulfur and fire') and the plagues (Exodus 9:18). The eating imagery is deliberate: the man who seized others' food will find that while eating, divine judgment falls on him.
24: yibrāḥ minnešeq bārzel tiḥᵉlᵉp̄ennû qešeṯ nᵉḥûšāh: 'He flees from the iron weapon, and the bronze bow strikes him through.' The double military imagery of flight and pursuit: even fleeing the iron weapon (neše) the man is struck by the bronze bow (qešeṯ nᵉḥûšāh). Inescapability of divine judgment is the theme — there is no direction to flee that avoids the sentence. The combination of two weapon-types (iron = short-range; bronze bow = long-range) suggests omnidirectional pursuit with no gap.
25: šālaph wayyēṣēʾ miggēwāh ûḇārāq mimmᵉrārāṯô yahalōk ʿālāyw ʾêmîm: 'The arrow is drawn; it comes out through his body; the gleaming blade exits through his gall; terrors (ʾêmîm) come upon him.' Bārāq (lightning bolt, gleaming metal) — the sword blade gleaming as it exits is a vivid martial image. Mᵉrōrāh (gall, bile) — the arrow or sword passes through the organ of bitterness; as the blade exits it draws out the gall that symbolizes his bitter accumulated evil. ʾÊmîm (terrors) — the same word used in 9:34 for Job's fear of God; here divine terrors accompany the physical death.
26: kōl-ḥōšeḵ ṣāpûn liṣᵉpûnāyw tᵉʾaḵlēhû ʾēš lōʾ-nuppāḥ yērāʿ śārîḏ bᵉʾohᵃlô: 'All darkness is stored up for his treasures; a fire not fanned by wind will consume him; disaster will destroy what remains in his tent.' Ḥōšeḵ ṣāpûn liṣᵉpûnāyw: 'darkness stored up for his stored-up things' — the wordplay (ṣāpûn twice) equates his treasure-hoarding with the darkness stored for his destruction: he stored, and darkness is stored for him. ʾĒš lōʾ-nuppāḥ (fire not blown upon) — spontaneous, self-sustaining divine fire, not human-kindled; the same quality as the fire of Sodom (Genesis 19:24).
27: yᵉgallû šāmayim ʿăwônô wᵉʾereṣ miṯqōmāmāh lô: 'The heavens will expose his guilt, and the earth will rise up against him.' The cosmic witness against the wicked: heaven above and earth below both testify to his iniquity. Gālāh (to expose, uncover, reveal) is the verb of the prophetic disclosure of hidden things (Amos 3:7: 'he reveals his secrets to his servants'). The earth 'rising' against him (qûm lô) personifies creation as judge — a motif seen in Isaiah 1:2 (heaven and earth as YHWH's witnesses) and Revelation 20:11 (the earth and heaven fleeing from the judgment throne).
28: yiggāl yᵉḇûl bêṯô niggārôṯ bᵉyôm ʾappô: 'The produce of his house will be carried away; his possessions will pour away in the day of his anger.' Nāgar (to flow, pour out) — liquid imagery for total dispersal of wealth: like water, it cannot be held. Yôm ʾappô (the day of his anger) — ambiguous pronoun: could be God's anger or the wicked man's anger. Most likely God's; 'the day of wrath' is standard eschatological-judgment language (Zephaniah 1:15; Proverbs 11:4). The house that symbolized his wealth and dynasty is emptied out.
29: zeh ḥēleq-ʾādām rāšāʿ mēʾĕlōhîm wᵉnaḥălat ʾimrô mēʾēl: 'This is the portion (ḥēleq) of the wicked man from God — the heritage (naḥălāh) decreed for him by God.' The summary verdict. Ḥēleq (portion, lot, assigned share) and naḥălāh (inheritance, heritage) are both used for Israel's covenantal inheritance of the land; here applied to the wicked man's 'inheritance' of judgment. The formulaic ending (mēʾēl = from God) makes the retribution explicitly theological: this is not mere natural consequence but divine assignment. Job will contest this portrait in Job 21 by pointing to observable reality.
